items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.summary: In this Feb 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
items.context: It connects media analysis with the original text published by The Moscow Times.

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.summary: This essay reevaluates the 2011 Bolotnaya protests not as a tactical failure, but as a structural inevitability. Framing the defeat as a Hegelian catalyst, it argues the subsequent crackdown inadvertently served as a "civil society university," transforming a naive public into a deeply politicized society primed for an unpredictable historical synthesis.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions
items.summary: This card summarizes "Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-08-24.
items.context: This piece places media freedom in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.

items.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
items.summary: How public-opinion narratives are shaped by institutions, incentives, and media distribution logic. This card summarizes "The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-07-12.
items.context: It connects public opinion with the original text published by The Moscow Times.

items.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
items.summary: A short summary of media ethics, with a direct link to the original publication.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
items.summary: How election-timing rhetoric can postpone decisions and normalize strategic waiting. This card summarizes "Let's Wait Until After the Elections" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-05-17.
items.context: It explains electoral timing and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
items.summary: The piece focuses on elite discourse and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2021.
items.context: This piece places elite discourse in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.

items.title: Bloomberg quotes Klishin on Russia’s Twitter crackdown
items.summary: This Bloomberg report covers Russia’s move to slow down Twitter as part of a broader crackdown on Western social platforms. Klishin is not the author, but he is quoted in the piece as it explains how the authorities were tightening pressure while still stopping short of a full block.
items.context: External feature card documenting how Bloomberg used Klishin commentary while reporting Russia’s platform pressure strategy.

items.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
items.summary: Which technical and political mechanisms can be used to restrict social-network access. This card summarizes "Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-02-12.
items.context: It connects social network regulation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.

items.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
items.summary: This The Moscow Times (2021) publication examines comparative media framing and summarizes the main argument.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
items.summary: How platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces. This card summarizes "Warning!
items.context: It explains disinformation and platforms and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Why Russians Dislike Good Russians in American Films No Less Than Bad Russians
items.summary: The article outlines cultural representation in clear terms and places it in editorial context.
items.context: It connects cultural representation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.

items.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
items.summary: How cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction. This card summarizes "Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King" published by The Moscow Times on 2020-11-09.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
items.summary: The piece focuses on platform influence and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2020.
items.context: It explains platform influence and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The
items.summary: Why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning. This card summarizes "Belarus Coverage Framing: The Moscow Times (2020-08-14) - Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The" published by The Moscow Times on 2020-08-14.
items.context: This piece places belarus coverage framing in a concrete editorial moment in 2020.

items.title: Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote
items.summary: The column explains how the pandemic disrupted a carefully staged constitutional plebiscite. Klishin argues that the Kremlin lost timing control and faced a strategic bind where postponement and continuation both carried rising political costs.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Why Medvedev's resignation and Putin's amendments changed nothing
items.summary: This Russian-language Moscow Times column argues that both events were staged inside a fixed power design. Klishin presents personnel rotation and constitutional theater as symbolic moves that changed optics but did not alter the real center of decision-making.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Who owns Russian? And who owns English?
items.summary: The column examines how language is politicized as an instrument of cultural control. Klishin compares claims over Russian and English to show that language policy often masks power struggles over identity, legitimacy, and who may define public norms.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him
items.summary: This Russian-language column frames financial pressure as a central tool of modern repression. Instead of visible violence, authorities can exhaust opponents through fines, frozen accounts, and legal costs while preserving the appearance of procedural legality.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition
items.summary: This English column describes bankruptcy tactics as a calibrated instrument of repression. Instead of spectacular crackdowns, the state can deplete opposition capacity through legal debts, penalties, and procedural pressure while preserving formal plausibility.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage
items.summary: Klishin connects two media agendas to show how distraction campaigns are built. The text argues that emotionally loaded topics can be amplified to redirect public attention and normalize selective information boundaries around politically sensitive narratives.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Russias New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are
items.summary: Klishin analyzes why plain factual speech became politically meaningful in a distorted information environment. The piece argues that when institutions fail, even minimal honesty from insiders can shift public attention and recalibrate legitimacy.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Why Russian Journalists Find Refuge in Propaganda Outlets
items.summary: The article examines structural reasons why independent journalists move into propaganda institutions. Klishin argues that contraction of the independent market and career precarity create conditions where ideological compromise becomes a professional survival strategy.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: The music of the media desert
items.summary: This Vedomosti column describes how shrinking pluralism produces a repetitive and hostile media environment. Klishin argues that the system rewards noise and intimidation, while public discussion loses nuance, memory, and professional standards.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Moscow: clean city, cleansed city
items.summary: The column uses urban cleanliness as a political metaphor for controlled public space. Klishin contrasts aesthetic order with social exclusion and argues that visible improvement can coexist with narrowed civic participation and managed dissent.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: From Kremlin Web Brigades to Russian Troll Factories
items.summary: The VSquare article tracks the evolution from early online brigades to industrialized troll operations and cites Klishin in that discussion. This card is included as an external analytical reference where his assessments are reused in broader reporting.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Private Authoritarianism: How Facebook and Google Threaten Democracy
items.summary: This Carnegie essay argues that major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. It examines how algorithmic ranking, moderation policies, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as strongly as state media controls. The text is included as an early framing of platform power as a governance problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
items.context: Strengthens the archive with an early argument about platform governance, accountability, and democratic risk beyond state media frameworks.

items.title: Web of Control: The Future of the Russian Internet
items.summary: This Carnegie analysis outlines scenarios for state control over the Russian internet. It tracks how policy, platform pressure, and institutional coordinators transformed once-unthinkable restrictions into routine options in public governance.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Do Revolutions Grow Out of Social Media?
items.summary: This Carnegie essay challenges the cliche that social networks directly produce revolutions. It argues that platforms can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion, but they do not replace institutions, organization, and political opportunity structures. Using post-Soviet examples, the text separates communication effects from causal claims about regime change and warns against technological determinism in media analysis.
items.context: Useful for separating media amplification from political causality in debates about digital protest dynamics across post-Soviet contexts.

items.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
items.summary: This column deconstructs the Kremlin bot infrastructure, framing it not as potent soft power, but as a reactive domestic tactic. Operating from a semantic void, these networks broadcast informational noise that merely exploits Western systemic anxieties rather than projecting genuine ideological influence.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Carnegie.

items.title: What Sparked Moldova's Second Maidan?
items.summary: This Carnegie analysis examines renewed protest mobilization in Moldova and asks why frustration returned after earlier electoral breakthroughs. The text tracks the gap between reform promises and lived outcomes, showing how repeated cycles of disappointment can reactivate street politics even when formal institutions remain unchanged. It is included as a regional case study of post-Soviet protest recurrence and narrative fatigue.
items.context: Adds a comparative regional case on protest recurrence, useful for interpreting delayed political effects after apparent electoral wins.

items.title: English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism
items.summary: How intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights reporting. This card summarizes "English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism" published by News24 on 2015-04-29.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Atlantic feature cites Klishin on Putin’s media machine
items.summary: This Atlantic feature looks at how the Russian media system was turned into a political weapon. Klishin is not the author, but the piece cites his analysis to explain the unequal conditions in which independent media were forced to operate.
items.context: External feature card showing how Klishin's analysis was used by a major English outlet to explain structural media pressure in Russia.

items.title: How Putin quietly took over Russian social media
items.summary: This essay examines how the Kremlin expanded its influence over Russian social media after the protest wave of 2011-2012. It follows the shift from crude bot activity to a more organized system of trolls, loyal networks, and pressure on independent media.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Global Voices source.

items.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
items.summary: This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
items.context: It explains war-reporting verification and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Likes Go West
items.summary: In a remarkably prescient May 2014 column for Vedomosti-published more than two full years before the 2016 US presidential election and the ensuing global scandal over Russian cyber interference-Klishin exposed the Kremlin’s nascent digital operations targeting Western democracies. Relying on exclusive sources close to the Presidential Administration, leaked files from "Anonymous International," and insights from European media executives, the piece detailed the strategic pivot of domestic troll farms toward American and European audiences. Klishin dismantled the emerging operational mindset of these state-sponsored networks.
items.context: It connects media analysis with the original text published by Vedomosti.

items.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
items.summary: This The Moscow Times (2014) publication examines disinformation analysis and summarizes the main argument.
items.context: It explains disinformation analysis and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Vojna Kotoruyu My Ne Zametili
items.summary: Part of the Maximum Retweet series, this text argues that information conflict had already become systemic before many audiences recognized it. Klishin links platform incentives, rapid amplification, and emotional framing to a new logic of political influence.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Maximum Retweet: Threats created by the Ministry of Truth
items.summary: This column examines how state-led narrative management can create risks for the system itself. Klishin describes escalation dynamics in which propaganda infrastructure grows faster than institutional control and begins producing unstable side effects.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Maximum Retweet: They purged the wrong people
items.summary: Klishin critiques selective crackdowns that target visible actors while leaving core mechanisms untouched. The text argues that cosmetic purges can harden the system, deepen cynicism, and fail to address the real architecture of manipulation.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Maximum Retweet: What happens to Runet after Crimea
items.summary: Published days after Crimea, the column maps scenarios for tightening internet control in Russia. Klishin outlines legal, technical, and political instruments that could shrink online autonomy and normalize emergency logic in everyday regulation.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Maximum Retweet: The web we got trapped in
items.summary: This early 2014 piece describes how legal registries, prosecutor powers, and platform pressure formed a practical censorship stack. Klishin shows that control expanded through routine procedures rather than a single dramatic ban.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: The internet will not do it automatically
items.summary: This 2013 Vedomosti text questions technological determinism in protest politics. Klishin argues that platforms can accelerate coordination, but durable civic change still depends on institutions, strategy, and offline organizational capacity.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Zero multiplied by a million
items.summary: One of Klishin's early columns on repetition and network amplification in propaganda. The argument is that low-quality signals can become socially powerful when multiplied across channels that manufacture visibility and perceived consensus.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
items.summary: This 2012 Human Rights Watch report chronicles the Kremlin’s strategic shift toward targeted pressure on protest organizers. A prime example of this repressive mechanic is the documented episode involving Ilya Klishin, the creator of the "For Fair Elections" network: operatives from the anti-extremism Center "E" interrogated his parents in Tambov, threatening them with vague extremism charges. The text vividly illustrates how authorities, spooked by grassroots mobilization, began deploying psychological blackmail—cynically redirecting their strikes from metropolitan activists to their vulnerable relatives in the provinces.
items.context: Reference card for a Human Rights Watch report documenting pressure tactics against protest organizers and their families during the post-2011 crackdown.

items.title: Russia's New Decembrists
items.summary: This New York Magazine feature on the 2011-2012 protest movement references Ilia Klishin within the organizing and media infrastructure around demonstrations. It is included as an external profile context source, not as an authored publication by Klishin.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.

items.summary: The article examines structural reasons why independent journalists move into propaganda institutions. Klishin argues that contraction of the independent market and career precarity create conditions where ideological compromise becomes a professional survival strategy.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
items.section_intro: Analysis of networked influence tactics, manipulation infrastructure, and platform adaptation.

items.title: From Kremlin Web Brigades to Russian Troll Factories
items.summary: The VSquare article tracks the evolution from early online brigades to industrialized troll operations and cites Klishin in that discussion. This card is included as an external analytical reference where his assessments are reused in broader reporting.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
items.section_intro: Analysis of networked influence tactics, manipulation infrastructure, and platform adaptation.

items.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
items.summary: This column deconstructs the Kremlin bot infrastructure, framing it not as potent soft power, but as a reactive domestic tactic. Operating from a semantic void, these networks broadcast informational noise that merely exploits Western systemic anxieties rather than projecting genuine ideological influence.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Carnegie.
items.section_intro: Analysis of networked influence tactics, manipulation infrastructure, and platform adaptation.

items.title: How Putin quietly took over Russian social media
items.summary: This essay examines how the Kremlin expanded its influence over Russian social media after the protest wave of 2011-2012. It follows the shift from crude bot activity to a more organized system of trolls, loyal networks, and pressure on independent media.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Global Voices source.
items.section_intro: Analysis of networked influence tactics, manipulation infrastructure, and platform adaptation.

items.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
items.summary: This The Moscow Times (2014) publication examines disinformation analysis and summarizes the main argument.
items.context: It explains disinformation analysis and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Vojna Kotoruyu My Ne Zametili
items.summary: Part of the Maximum Retweet series, this text argues that information conflict had already become systemic before many audiences recognized it. Klishin links platform incentives, rapid amplification, and emotional framing to a new logic of political influence.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Zero multiplied by a million
items.summary: One of Klishin's early columns on repetition and network amplification in propaganda. The argument is that low-quality signals can become socially powerful when multiplied across channels that manufacture visibility and perceived consensus.
items.context: Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.

items.title: Why Russians Dislike Good Russians in American Films No Less Than Bad Russians

items.title: English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism
items.summary: How intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights reporting. This card summarizes "English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism" published by News24 on 2015-04-29.

items.section_intro: Third-party publications and institutional references used as external context.

items.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
items.summary: This 2012 Human Rights Watch report chronicles the Kremlin’s strategic shift toward targeted pressure on protest organizers. A prime example of this repressive mechanic is the documented episode involving Ilya Klishin, the creator of the "For Fair Elections" network: operatives from the anti-extremism Center "E" interrogated his parents in Tambov, threatening them with vague extremism charges. The text vividly illustrates how authorities, spooked by grassroots mobilization, began deploying psychological blackmail—cynically redirecting their strikes from metropolitan activists to their vulnerable relatives in the provinces.
items.context: Reference card for a Human Rights Watch report documenting pressure tactics against protest organizers and their families during the post-2011 crackdown.
items.section_intro: Third-party publications and institutional references used as external context.
